coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence switching sensor for sensitive detection of curcumin/Mn2+ using biomass carbon quantum dots: A combination of experimental and theoretical insights

coredata.dc:description: Compared to high-performance liquid chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, and electrochemical analysis, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs)derived from biomass materials have garnered significant attention due to their non-toxic, eco-friendly, and renewable advantages. In this research, CQDs-NH2 was synthesized in one step by hydrothermal method using banana peel as biomass carbon source and ethylenediamine (EDA) as dopant. The blue fluorescence of CQDs-NH2 was found to be quenched by the addition of curcumin through the synergistic effect of static quenching and internal filtering effect (IFE). The response to curcumin showed good linearity in the concentration range of 1–15 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 18.56 nM. The fluorescence of CQDs-NH2 was partially restored in the presence of curcumin and Mn2+, leading to the establishment of a curcumin@CQDs-NH2 switch sensor for the detection of Mn2+ in the linear range of 5–60 μM with a LOD of 20.35 nM. Furthermore, density functional theory (DFT) calculations suggest that the interaction between Mn2+ and curcumin is stronger than the interaction between Mn2+ and functionalized CQDs (f-CQDs), elucidating the introduction of Mn2+ disrupts the interaction between curcumin and CQDs-NH2, leading to the restoration of CQDs-NH2 fluorescence. Finally, the CQDs-NH2 fluorescence switch sensor demonstrated high accuracy and precision in liquid food and water quality testing with recoveries ranging from 95.19 % to 105.54 %.

The binding energy (Eb), charge transfers (CT) and topological parameters (au) at BCP of interaction for curcumin-Mn2+, complexⅠ, complexⅡ and complexⅢ.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence switching sensor for sensitive detection of curcumin/Mn2+ using biomass carbon quantum dots: A combination of experimental and theoretical insights

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Ya’an 625014, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Ya’an 625014, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Compared to high-performance liquid chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, and electrochemical analysis, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs)derived from biomass materials have garnered significant attention due to their non-toxic, eco-friendly, and renewable advantages. In this research, CQDs-NH2 was synthesized in one step by hydrothermal method using banana peel as biomass carbon source and ethylenediamine (EDA) as dopant. The blue fluorescence of CQDs-NH2 was found to be quenched by the addition of curcumin through the synergistic effect of static quenching and internal filtering effect (IFE). The response to curcumin showed good linearity in the concentration range of 1–15 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 18.56 nM. The fluorescence of CQDs-NH2 was partially restored in the presence of curcumin and Mn2+, leading to the establishment of a curcumin@CQDs-NH2 switch sensor for the detection of Mn2+ in the linear range of 5–60 μM with a LOD of 20.35 nM. Furthermore, density functional theory (DFT) calculations suggest that the interaction between Mn2+ and curcumin is stronger than the interaction between Mn2+ and functionalized CQDs (f-CQDs), elucidating the introduction of Mn2+ disrupts the interaction between curcumin and CQDs-NH2, leading to the restoration of CQDs-NH2 fluorescence. Finally, the CQDs-NH2 fluorescence switch sensor demonstrated high accuracy and precision in liquid food and water quality testing with recoveries ranging from 95.19 % to 105.54 %.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin is an active ingredient extracted from the rhizome of turmeric and plays important pharmacological roles in anti-inflammatory, anticancer, antioxidant and antibacterial properties [1]. In addition, curcumin has been classified as a natural additive by the World Health Organization (WHO) and the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [2]. However, it has been shown that excessive intake of curcumin leads to antioxidant effects on deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and reduces adenosine triphosphate (ATP) in the cells, which ultimately leads to normal cellular damage or necrosis [3]. Therefore, it is crucial to accurately quantify the amount of curcumin in food or other products to ensure that the daily intake of curcumin is within a reasonable range, given the wide range of applications of curcumin in various fields.

Besides curcumin, metal ions play an important role in people's life activities. Manganese (Mn2+) is abundant in the earth's crust and is found throughout the soil, water bodies and industrial environments, and is widely used in wastewater treatment, battery manufacturing and mining. It is also a key cofactor in the function of biological enzymes, but exposure to high doses can cause toxicity, affecting neurological, cardiac and pulmonary health [4]. The human body's need for Mn2+ comes mainly from water, which contains large amounts of manganese [5]. Therefore, the development of an efficient and accurate analytical method to measure Mn2+ in water is essential to protect human health.

Various methods have been developed for detecting curcumin or Mn2+, including liquid chromatography [6], electrode analysis [7], colorimetry [8], and fluorescence sensing [9]. Among them, fluorescence methods are widely used for molecular and ion detection due to its good selectivity, high sensitivity, and better performance than other methods [10,11]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have recently gained popularity as nanomaterials for fluorescence analysis and detection due to their abundant carbon source, easy synthesis, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and optical properties [12]. Nowadays, CQDs have been developed for detecting curcumin or Mn2+. For example, He et al. developed a highly selective fluorescence method based on hydrophobic CQDs in linseed oil for the specific determination of curcumin with a detection limit of 20 nM [13]. Liu et al. synthesized blue-green-emitting fluorescent CQDs as nanoprobes for the rapid detection of curcumin in food samples [14]. Xu et al. synthesised selenium and nitrogen co-doped CQDs for Mn2+ detection, which showed good stability, sensitivity, selectivity and reproducibility [15]. Li et al. proposed N-CQDs that can specifically respond to Mn2+ with a minimum concentration of 5 nM, achieving visible warming of Mn2+ above drinking water standards [16]. However, there are almost no CQDs that can detect both curcumin and Mn2+. Therefore, it is exciting to develop new CQDs for the simultaneous detection of curcumin and Mn2+.

The current methods for synthesizing CQDs include thermal decomposition, hydrothermal synthesis, electrochemical oxidation, and microwave-assisted methods. Hydrothermal synthesis is popular due to its environmental friendliness and ease of mass production [17,18]. CQDs made from biomass materials are cost-effective, readily available, and environmentally friendly, adding value to natural materials [19]. Among them, banana peels are an excellent sustainable source for the production of CQDs due to their extremely high carbohydrate content [20,21]. The surface properties of CQDs greatly depend on their surface states, and surface functionalization is commonly used to modify these properties [22]. Our research group studied the electronic transitions of pristine and surface-functionalized CQDs (f-CQDs, f=-OH, -NH2, -COOH) using density-functional theory (DFT) calculations. Our findings suggest that functionalization reduces the energy gap values of CQDs, with the lowest energy gap value observed for CQDs-NH2 [23]. It is proposed that the additional energy bands in CQDs with lower energy gap values lead to increased fluorescence luminescence processes [24]. Consequently, it can be concluded that CQDs-NH2 exhibits the best fluorescence performance.

Herein, CQDs-NH2 with enhanced fluorescence performance is prepared by hydrothermal synthesis using banana peel as a carbon source and ethylenediamine(EDA) as an amino dopant for the detection of curcumin. It is found that curcumin effectively reduces the fluorescence of CQDs-NH2 through internal filtration effect (IFE) and static quench. Furthermore, a curcumin@CQDs-NH2 sensor can detect Mn2+ through a fluorescence switch mechanism, where the fluorescence of CQDs-NH2 is partially restored by the weakening of IFE and static quench due to the formation of a complex between Mn2+ and curcumin ( Fig. 1). Finally, the interaction between curcumin and CQDs-NH2 with Mn2+ is investigated by DFT calculations, which explain the sensing mechanism of the fluorescent switching sensors and is applied to the detection of real samples.

Banana peels were obtained from the waste of fruit stores in Ya'an, Sichuan Province, China. KCl, NaCl, NH4Cl, BaCl2·2H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, LiCl, SnCl2·2H2O, CrCl3, CoCl2·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, ZnCl2, MgCl2·6H2O, CaCl2, AlCl3·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, CdCl2, Pb(NO3)2 were purchased from Chengdu Kelon Reagent Co. Glutamic acid, serine, alanine, glycine, methionine, aspartic acid, phenylalanine, lysine, histidine, threonine, tyrosine, tryptophan, and arginine were purchased from McLean Biochemicals Ltd, Shanghai, China. Curcumin and EDA were purchased from Chengdu Hoboyo Technology Co. All the above reagents were analytically pure and did not require further purification. Deionized water was used for all routine experiments, and BR (Britton-Robinson) buffer (prepared with 0.04 mol/L H3BO3, H3PO4, and CH3COOH, and adjusted to the desired pH with 0.2 mol/L NaOH solution on an acidometer when used) was used in the experiments.

The synthesis of CQDs-NH2 was carried out using an electrically heated blast drying oven (101–00B, Shanghai, China) and a hydrothermal reactor (KH-100, Zhejiang, China). A UV-1600PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai Meppan Instrument Co., Ltd., China) was used for UV analysis. All fluorescence analyses in this paper were performed using an LS-55 (PerkinElmer, USA) spectrophotometer. The crystalline phase of CQDs-NH2 was analyzed by a DX-2700B (Fangyuan Instrument Co., Ltd., Dandong, China) x-ray diffractometer (XRD), and the microstructure of CQDs-NH2 was characterized by a JEOL-2100f (JEOL, Japan) transmission electron microscope (TEM) at different magnifications. The elemental composition and surface groups were analyzed by K-Alpha (Thermo, USA) x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Nicolet Is50 (Themor, USA) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), respectively. Quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime were measured using a transient steady-state spectrometer from FLS1000 (Edinburgh, UK).

CQDs-NH2 was prepared by hydrothermal method using banana peels as biomass carbon source and adding EDA. Typically, fresh banana peels discarded from fruit salvage stores were dried at 50 °C for 48 h and then ground into powder using a pulverizer to filter out the homogeneous fine powder through a sieve. Then 1 g of banana peel powder was weighed, 40 ml of deionized water and 200 µL EDA were added, mixed well, and poured into a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and reacted for 24 h at 220 °C in an oven. It was naturally cooled to room temperature, and the supernatant was purified by passing it through a cellulose filter membrane with a pore size of 0.22 µm and dialyzed with a dialysis bag (molecular weight threshold of 500 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the brownish-yellow solution of CQDs-NH2 was collected and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C protected from light for use. The solid CQDs used for characterization were obtained by drying a CQDs solution in an oven at 60 °C for 48 h. To obtain materials with the best luminescence properties, experiments were carried out to optimize the synthesis conditions (see Fig. S1 in Supplementary material).

To test the detection range of curcumin, different volumes of curcumin solution (600 µmol/L) were added to 30 μL of CQDs-NH2 solution (pH=7) in a total volume of 3 ml. The fluorescence of the sensing system of curcumin@CQDs-NH2 (100 μL, 600 µmol/L curcumin, 30 μL CQDs-NH2 in BR buffer, pH=9) was added with 100 μL of Mn2+ solution of different concentrations in a total volume of 3 ml to monitor the fluorescence recovery of the sensing system of curcumin@CQDs-NH2. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, an excitation slit of 15 nm, and an emission slit of 5 nm. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs-NH2 decreased with the increase of the curcumin concentration, and the relationship between the two could be fitted by using the Stern-Volmer equation [25]:

DFT calculations were performed using the ORCA program [27] to optimize adsorption structures. All calculations were performed using the PBE0 functional [28] combined with the def2-SVP basis set [29], and the DFT-D3 method [30] was used to consider the van der Waals (vdW) interactions. The quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) and atomic dipole moment corrected Hirschfeld (ADCH) charge and electron density differences were analyzed using the Multiwfn wavefunction program [31] to reveal interaction characteristics. To study the binding strengths of Mn2+ (MnCl2•H2O) with curcumin and functionalized CQDs, we defined the binding energy (E b) of the complexes as follows [32]:
(3)

where E complex is the total energy of the complex, E curcumin/CQDs is the energy of individual curcumin or CQDs, respectively, and E Mn 2+ is the energy of individual MnCl2•H2O, and the basis set superposition error (BSSE) [33] was corrected in the binding energy calculation.

The TEM image in Fig. 2a revealed that the CQDs-NH2 exhibited spherical and uniformly distributed, with particle sizes in the range of 1.5–3.5 nm and an average particle size of 2.42 nm. In the HR-TEM image inset of Fig. 2b, the CQDs-NH2 exhibited a well-defined lattice structure with clear lattice stripes. The measured lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitized carbon[34]. The XRD pattern of CQDs-NH2 was shown in Fig. 2c, with a diffraction peak at 2θ of 23.6°, corresponding to a d-spacing of 0.37 nm, which was a planar layer spacing slightly larger than that of typical bulk graphite (∼0.34 nm). This confirms that the synthesised CQDs-NH2 is predominantly amorphous in structure, which may be due to the surface functionality of the synthesised CQDs-NH2 [35].

The FTIR spectrum in Fig. 2d revealed the surface functionalization of CQDs-NH2. There were broad peaks in the 3270–3360 cm−1 band, indicating the stretching vibrations of O-H (3360 cm−1) and NH2 (3270 cm−1) [36], confirming the successful synthesis of the amino-functionalized CQDs. Additionally, the C-H stretching vibrations were observed at 2930 cm−1 [37]. Furthermore, the peaks near 1640, 1580, 1140, and 1040 cm−1 represented the stretching vibrations of CO, CC, C-OH, and C-O-C, respectively [38,39]. The absorption peaks near 1380 and 1450 cm−1 denoted the stretching vibration of C-N-C [35] and C-N [40], respectively. Moreover, the peak observed at 773 cm−1 represented =C-H symmetric bending vibration [35]. Thus, the doping of EDA successfully constructed amino-functionalized CQDs.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the surface properties of CQDs-NH2. The full-scan XPS spectrum was presented in Fig. 3. In Fig. 3a, the XPS spectrum shows peaks at around 285, 400, and 532 eV, indicating the presence of the elements C, N, and O, with atomic ratios of 74.3 %, 8.1 %, and 17.6 % respectively. This suggests that the surface of CQDs-NH2 is predominantly composed of carbon, with some oxygen and nitrogen. Additionally, in Fig. 3b, the high-resolution XPS in the C1 band reveals peaks at 284.8, 286.1, and 288.1 eV, which are attributed to C-C/CC, C-O-C, and -CONH- bonds, respectively [41]. The high-resolution XPS in the N1 showed three peaks at 398.8, 399.9, and 400.9 eV corresponding to the (C)3-N OC-NH, C-NH2 [42] in Fig. 3c, and the three fitted peaks at 531.1, 532.1, and 532.7 eV in Fig. 3d were attributed to CO, C-OH/C-O-C, and OC-OH, respectively [39]. The XPS mapping analysis confirms the presence of nitrogen-containing -NH2 and oxygen-containing -COOH, -OH groups in the synthesized CQDs-NH2, consistent with the FTIR spectra. In conclusion, the synthesized CQDs-NH2 exhibit good water solubility and dispersibility due to the presence of these functional groups.

As shown in Fig. 4a, the UV–visible absorption peaks of CQDs-NH2 are observed near 274 nm and 334 nm. The first peak corresponds to the π-π* transition of the CC structure, and the second peak corresponds to the n-π* transition of the CN structure [43,44]. It has been confirmed that n-π* leaps can cause fluorescence enhancement [45]. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CQDs-NH2 is 8.64 %, which is higher than the fluorescence quantum yield of non-EDA banana peel fluorescent CQDs synthesized under the same conditions, which is 4.78 %. This indicates that the fluorescence performance of CQDs functionalized with N-groups has been improved. Additionally, the quantum yield of CQDs-NH2 prepared was higher than previously reported, 1 % [46], 7 % [47]. As shown in Fig. 4b, the emission behavior of CQDs-NH2 depended on the excitation wavelength, and the emission peaks shifted from 434 nm to 482 nm when the excitation wavelengths were changed from 300 nm to 410 nm. The excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior of CQDs-NH2 may be due to the difference in the surface luminescence sites [48]. In addition, it can be concluded that the peak emission reaches its maximum when the excitation wavelength is 360 nm. The stability of CQDs-NH2 was better in different concentrations of NaCl solution, UV irradiation time, storage time and temperature (see Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linearity and selectivity of CQDs-NH2 as fluorescent sensors for the detection of curcumin
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the sensitivity of CQDs-NH2 to curcumin, different concentrations of curcumin (1–25 μM, Fig. 5a) were added to the CQDs-NH2 fluorescent probe under the optimal assay conditions (see Fig. S3(a–c) in Supplementary material). The fluorescence quenching rate reached 87.96 %, indicating a decline in fluorescence intensity with increasing curcumin concentration. The regression equation was fitted as F0/F=0.1649Ccurcumin+0.8079, with a linear correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9933 and a limit of detection (LOD) of 18.56 nM in Fig. 5b. Furthermore, Table 1 presented a comparison of various CQDs-based probes for detecting curcumin, which was relevant to our work. The broader linear range and lower LOD of our designed fluorescent sensor suggested that CQDs-NH2 offers high sensitivity, low cost, and non-toxicity, making it a promising luminescent sensor for the future.

The selectivity of CQDs-NH2 for curcumin was further investigated by comparing the fluorescence intensities under 360 nm excitation after adding 10 μM curcumin and 30 μM concentrations of various metal ions (K+, Na+, NH4 +, Ba2+, Cu2+, Li+, Sn2+, Cr3+, Co2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Pb2+) and amino acid molecules (from left to right, alanine, serine, glycine, methionine, aspartic acid, phenylalanine, lysine, histidine, threonine, tyrosine, glutamic acid, tryptophan, and arginine). The results (Fig. 5(c-d)) showed that curcumin significantly quenched the fluorescence of CQDs-NH2 by 66.34 %, while other metal ions and amino acid molecules had less effect, indicating that CQDs-NH2 has greater selectivity for curcumin than for other competing ions and small molecule compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linearity and selectivity of curcumin@CQDs-NH2 as fluorescent sensors for the detection of Mn2+
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of detecting curcumin and utilizing curcumin as a mediator for the detection of Mn2+ was evaluated through the utilization of a fluorescence spectrophotometer, as illustrated in Fig. S4 (See Supplementary material). The study found that curcumin could quench the fluorescence of CQDs-NH2, but when Mn2+ was introduced to CQDs-NH2 alone, the fluorescence intensity remained unchanged. However, in the curcumin@CQDs-NH2 sensing system, Mn2+ was able to restore some of the fluorescence of CQDs-NH2. The study demonstrated that the CQDs-NH2 sensor could determine both curcumin and Mn2+.

In order to assess the efficacy of the curcumin@CQDs-NH2 sensing system for the quantitative detection of the target ion Mn2+, the fluorescence response of CQDs-NH2 to varying concentrations of Mn2+ was examined under optimal detection conditions (See Fig. S3(d-f) in Supplementary material). It is noteworthy that Fig. S3e shows that the curcumin@CQDs-NH2 sensing system is reddish brown in colour at pH 9 and changes to light yellow on addition of Mn2+. The reason for this change is that curcumin automatically converts to keto-enol interconversion isomer under alkaline conditions, which forms a stable complex with Mn2+ [54,55]. Therefore, at pH 9, curcumin can react with Mn2+ to form a complex that restores the fluorescence of CQDs-NH2. Therefore, pH 9 is the optimum value for the detection of Mn2+ based on colour change, intensity of fluorescence recovery and alkaline environment. The results ( Fig. 6a) showed a significant increase in fluorescence intensity as the Mn2+ concentration increased from 0 to 60 μM, indicating that Mn2+ could dissociate the curcumin@CQDs-NH2 complex, leading to a partial fluorescence recovery of CQDs-NH2. In addition, the linear equation of Mn2+ was ΔF=5.0064CMn 2+-3.1991, with a linear range of 5–60 μΜ, an R2 value of 0.9947, and a calculated LOD of 20.35 nM (Fig. 6b). In addition, Table 2 compares various intentional fluorescent sensors based on CQDs for the detection of Mn2+. The curcumin@CQDs-NH2 fluorescent sensor designed in this study has a wider linear range and lower LOD than CQDs alone, which suggests that curcumin@CQDs-NH2 has a promising future as an turn-on sensor for Mn2+.

Furthermore, the anti-interference ability of the curcumin@CQDs-NH2 sensing system was further investigated, and the highest recovery of Mn2+ was achieved by recording the emission intensities of curcumin@CQDs-NH2 with various metal ions and amino acid molecules under 360 nm excitation (Fig. 6(c-d)). This research demonstrates that CQDs-NH2 can be a reliable liquid fluorescence sensor for analyzing Mn2+.

In Fig. 7a, it appears that curcumin quenches the fluorescence of CQDs-NH2 through an inner filter effect (IFE) [57], as the absorption spectrum of curcumin overlaps with the blue emission and excitation peaks of CQDs-NH2. Besides, UV spectra of CQDs-NH2, curcumin solution and their mixture were also recorded. The addition of curcumin resulted in a slight red shift of the absorption peak of CQDs-NH2 at 334 nm, with the characteristic absorption peak of curcumin appearing at 422 nm (Fig. 7b). Using DFT calculation, our research group found that curcumin forms covalent bonds with the amino groups of CQDs-NH2 [23]. This indicates that an interaction between curcumin and the amino functional groups on the surface of CQDs-NH2 forms a non-luminous complex, leading to static quenching [58,59]. This conclusion is supported by fluorescence lifetime measurements (Fig. 7c), which showed no significant change in the average fluorescence lifetime of CQDs-NH2 (9.66 ns) in the presence of curcumin (9.64 ns), confirming the static quenching mechanism. In conclusion, the fluorescence quenching of CQDs-NH2 by curcumin in this study is likely a result of both static quenching and the IFE.

Since CQDs-NH2 can quantitatively analyse curcumin and Mn2+, the detection mechanism of CQDs-NH2 as a sensor for Mn2+ and curcumin-Mn2+ complexes was investigated. As shown in Fig. 8a, the addition of Mn2+ to the curcumin@CQDs-NH2 sensing system resulted in a slight blue displacement of the characteristic peak of curcumin along with a decrease in the absorbance intensity. This displacement could be ascribed to the formation of the curcumin-Mn2+ complex. In addition, the absorption peak of curcumin at 260 nm was red-shifted to 264 nm, accompanied by an increase in absorption intensity. The addition of Mn2+ to the curcumin@CQDs-NH2 system decreased and increased the absorption peaks of curcumin at 422 and 260 nm, respectively, reducing the spectral overlap and disturbing the IFE relationship. Nevertheless, it can be seen from Fig. 8a that there is still a small overlap between the absorption spectrum of curcumin-Mn2+ and the fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of CQDs-NH2, and therefore, there was still the process of IFE, which made that the fluorescence of CQDs-NH2 had not been recovered completely. The partial recovery of CQDs-NH2 fluorescence may be due to the partial dissociation of curcumin in the curcumin@CQDs-NH2 complex by the addition of Mn2+, and the dissociated curcumin generated a complex with Mn2+, and the static quenching effect was weakened. It has been reported that curcumin can easily chelate Mn2+ ions to form complexes and scavenge reactive radicals [55]. We therefore further tracked curcumin-Mn2+ complex formation by fluorescence recovery, and the dosage in the curcumin@CQDs-NH2 sensing system was kept at 30 μL of CQDs-NH2 and 100 μL of curcumin (20 μM). The effect of the curcumin-Mn2+ complex on the fluorescence recovery of CQDs-NH2 was shown in Fig. 8b. The highest fluorescence recovery (52 %) was achieved at the curcumin-Mn2+ complex ratio of 3:1, after which the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-NH2 remained constant as the Mn2+ concentration increased. In summary, the addition of Mn2+ weakened the IFE and static quench between curcumin and CQDs-NH2, which disrupted their interactions, thus allowing the fluorescence of CQDs-NH2 to be partially restored. CQDs-NH2 can be used as a fluorescent sensor for curcumin, Mn2+ and to track the formation of curcumin-Mn2+ complexes.

To understand the sensing mechanism of CQDs-NH2 as a fluorescent switching sensor for curcumin and Mn2+, the DFT theoretical calculations were performed. The optimized structures of isolated curcumin, Mn2+ (MnCl2∙H2O), and functionalized CQDs (f-CQDs) were shown in Fig. S5(a–c). The molecular structure of the CQDs used in our study was a carbon cluster model (C54H18), in which two hydrogen atoms at the edges of the CQDs were replaced by -OH, -NH2, and -COOH groups, respectively, to construct the f-CQDs (see Supporting Material Fig. S5(b)). The bonding between curcumin and Mn2+ was formed through two Mn-O chemical bonds as shown in Fig. S5(d). Due to the presence of three functional groups in f-CQDs, we considered three adsorption configurations of Mn2+ interacting with these three functional groups (-OH, -NH2, and -COOH), and the relaxed adsorption configurations are shown in Fig. S5(e-g), where I was the complex of Mn2+ with the -OH adsorption site in f-CQDs, II represented the complex of Mn2+ with the -NH2 adsorption sited in f-CQDs, and III represented the complex of Mn2+ with the -COOH adsorption siteed in f-CQDs. It has been stated that the more negative the value of Eb, the stronger the adsorption and the easier it was to form stable complexes [60]. As shown in Table 3, the Eb values of all the complexes studied were negative, proving that they were exothermic reactions. In addition, the Eb with BSSE correction and Eb of curcumin@Mn2+ were significantly greater than that of the complexⅠ, Ⅱ, Ⅲ as can be seen in Fig. S6. Our previous DFT calculations show that the binding energies of curcumin with different f-CQDs are in the range of −0.95 ∼ −1.25 eV [23]. Based on the binding energies, it can be concluded that the interaction between Mn2+ and curcumin was stronger than the interaction between f-CQDs and Mn2+ and between curcumin and f-CQDs, respectively, thus the curcumin-Mn2+ complex was more inclined to be formed. As shown in Table 3, the charge transfer (CT) values from curcumin to Mn2+ were still higher than those from f-CQDs to Mn2+, which agreed with the binding energy results.

The QTAIM analyses were performed to further compare the bonding strengths in the studied complexes. Fig. 9 shows the bond critical points (BCPs) and bond paths between Mn2+ and curcumin or f-CQDs. Table 3 presents the topological parameters related to Mn-O or Mn-N BCPs. The ∇2ρ and H(r) for all BCP bonding of complex I were positive and -V(r)/G(r) was between 0 and 1 with ρ values less than 0.1 au, suggesting that the interactions of Mn2+ with the f-CQDs adsorbed configuration I were all noncovalent and weak van der Waals interactions [61]. The -V(r)/G(r) of the H-Cl bond in curcumin-Mn2+, complex II and complex III were between 1 and 2, and H(r) was negative, indicating that electrostatic interactions and covalent interactions played a significant role in the H-Cl bond [62]. In contrast, the other bonds of these three complexes were in non-covalent weak van der Waals interactions as in complex I. Generally, for similar chemical bonds, larger ρ(r) and H(r) values indicate stronger bonds [63]. Among the three chemical bonds with H-Cl-like chemical bonds, the curcumin-Mn2+ complex had the largest value of ρ(r) and H(r). Likewise, in the curcumin-Mn2+ complex, complex I, and complex III with O-Mn bonds, the ρ(r) value of the curcumin-Mn2+ complex is the highest. Previous research in our group has analyzed curcumin@f-CQDs using QTAIM and symmetry-adapted perturbation theory (SAPT) between covalent interactions and electrostatic interactions. However, the values of ρ(r) and H(r) were found to be weaker for covalent interactions between them, and dispersion forces are shown to play a dominant role in stabilization [23].

The charge density differences (CDD) of the four complexes were examined, which were positive in cyan blue and negative in orange, as shown in Fig. 10. It was well known that the formation of a covalent bond was accompanied by the transfer of electrons to the bonding region, which was a necessary condition for the formation of a covalent bond between two atoms. Therefore, a significant increase in electron density in the bonding region between H and Cl atoms can be detected in the CDD plots of curcumin-Mn2+, complex II, and complex III, with the electron density in the bonding region of curcumin-Mn2+ being greater. In conclusion, both covalent and non-covalent interactions of Mn2+ with curcumin were verified to be stronger than the interaction of f-CQDs with Mn2+ by theoretical calculations. Additionally, the covalent interaction of Mn2+ with curcumin was stronger than the interaction of f-CQDs with curcumin, as confirmed by existing studies in our group [23]. Thus, it explained the formation of curcumin-Mn2+ complexes in the experiment, leading to the partial dissociation of curcumin@CQDs-NH2, the weakening of IFE and static quench, and the restoration of the fluorescence of CQDs-NH2.

To assess the reliability of CQDs-NH2 as a biomass fluorescence sensor in real samples, spiking recovery experiments were performed on curcumin and Mn2+ content in liquid food and water samples. The spike recovery experiments were conducted by adding known quantities of curcumin or Mn2+ to the liquid food and water sample extracts. As illustrated in Table 4, the spike recoveries exhibited a range of 95.19–105.54 %, with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) falling below 5.5 % (n = 3), which indicated that the established analytical methods had high accuracy and precision. The fluorescence sensing system exhibited high sensitivity and good selectivity, rendering it a promising approach to effectively detecting curcumin and Mn2+.

In this study, banana peel (carbon source) and ethylenediamine (nitrogen source) were used as raw materials to prepare CQDs-NH2 using the green hydrothermal method. A simple, cost-effective, and sensitive optical nano switch was developed for detecting curcumin and the metal ion Mn2+. When curcumin solution was added to CQDs-NH2, the blue fluorescence of CQDs-NH2 was quenched, while the addition of Mn2+ restored the fluorescence of the system. The UV absorption peak indicated that the amino groups on the surface of carbon quantum dots form a complex with curcumin, leading to fluorescence quenching. The linear range for detecting curcumin is 1–15 μM, with a LOD of 18.56 nM. Additionally, DFT studies revealed that the interaction between Mn2+ and curcumin is stronger than that between Mn2+ and f-CQDs. Therefore, the addition of Mn2+ partially dissociates curcumin @ CQDs-NH2, thus restoring the fluorescence of CQDs-NH2. The linear range for Mn2+ is 5–60 μM, with a LOD of 20.35 nM. Finally, the sensor was used to detect water quality in food and the environment, demonstrating good accuracy. In summary, this study not only enhances the detection techniques for curcumin and Mn2+ but also provides insights into sensing mechanisms and improvements in sensor performance.

Longyan Yang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Kun Huang: Formal analysis. Xinyu Li: Investigation. Yi Zhang: Investigation. Pengfei Yin: Investigation. Xiyuan Sun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization..
